title: This town ran out of fuel days ago. No one knows when more will arrive
text:
Service stations across Australia have been hit by fuel shortages, leading to panic buying and queues of cars around the block in some places.

But there are no lines of locals hoping to fill up jerry cans at the independent ﻿Batlow petrol station﻿ – because it's clean out of fuel.

The only servo in the town more than 400km from Sydney ran out of petrol earlier this week and no one knows when more will arrive.

Batlow service station, which has run out of fuel. (Facebook/Dr Joe McGirr MP)

"We've got no fuel, and the operator hasn't got any and can't give us any sort of date when the fuel is going to come back," Batlow service station worker Sam Hughes told nine.com.au.

He said operators are having a hard time finding fuel at a reasonable price, leaving independent stations like Batlow in strife.﻿

"This is the environment where independents get squeezed out of markets﻿," he said.

"We're waiting to see if the market comes down or we get some sort of government assistance."

READ MORE: Rain set to continue in wet start to autumn across parts of country

Member for Wagga Wagga Dr Joe McGirr echoed Hughes' fear that independent retailers are being squeezed out by big companies, leaving communities like Batlow to pay the price.

"I'm hearing of independent operators under quite a lot of pressure, not getting fuel, running low on fuel, even running out of fuel elsewhere too," he told nine.com.au.

"That says to me, there is an issue with the system, this is not just panic buying.

"Batlow is a bit like the canary in the coal mine﻿."

Member for Wagga Wagga Dr Joe McGirr stands by Batlow service station. (Facebook/Dr Joe McGirr MP)

The fuel shortage caused by the war in Iran has hit regional areas especially hard, as most have access to a limited number of service stations but rely on petrol for more than just getting around.

Plenty of Batlow locals own acreages and need fuel for tractors and other machinery as well as their cars.

It's also vital to the local agriculture industry, as well as emergency services in the region.

﻿With the local servo all sold out, Batlow residents are being forced to drive 30 minutes away to Tumut for reliable fuel.

But if you've only got a quarter tank left, and there's no public transport, that's a big ask.

"It's been a shock to the local population, especially in the lower socio-economic [bracket]," Hughes said.

﻿"Hopefully there's a bit of community spirit when it comes to jerry canning around."

He added that Australians living in metropolitan areas don't understand just how hard the fuel shortage ﻿has hit regional areas.

Because it's not just Batlow struggling; it's small towns across the entire nation.

And the shortage could have a significant long-term impact on those local economies.

READ MORE: Search underway for man who vanished off a houseboat amid Queensland floods

"In regional areas, agriculture and truck transport are the lifeline of our economy," McGirr said.

"They're important for all of our wealth, so our focus on that is going to be pretty critical in the months ahead."

Today, Energy Minister Chris Bowen﻿ announced the government will help address the fuel shortage by releasing up to 20 per cent of its local "rainy day" stockpiles for petrol and diesel.

Chris Bowen today announced relief is on the way to regional areas. (Alex Ellinghausen)

It will free up more than 760 million litres of fuel from the nation's domestic reserves.

Bowen said the government is also taking steps to ﻿prioritise supply to regional areas hit hardest by the shortage.

In the meantime, McGirr and Hughes said Batlow is a resilient community and will rally together until more fuel arrives.

When exactly that will be, however, remains unclear.﻿

title: Bank jumps the gun with fixed rate hike before predicted RBA double blow
text:
A﻿NZ is the latest lender to raise its fixed mortgage rates in anticipation of the Reserve Bank's interest rate decision next week.

The big four bank moved to raise fixed﻿ rates by 0.25 percentage points in an early blow to borrowers ahead of what is expected to be a double RBA rate hike in March and April.

This makes ANZ's most affordable fixed-rate 5.99 per cent for o﻿ne-year terms, 6.04 per cent for two years and 6.14 per cent for three-year terms.

A﻿NZ is the latest lender to raise its fixed mortgage rates in anticipation of the RBA's next cash rate decision. (Will Willitts)

ANZ is not alone in jumping the gun with a tactical fixed-rate hike before the RBA makes its move on March 17.

According to Canstar, 26 lenders have hiked at least one fixed rate in the last two weeks.

Among them are Bankwest, Ubank, Heritage Bank and RACQ.﻿

The average two-year fixed rate is now 0.21 percentage points higher than the average variable rate, Canstar research found.

Thhe RBA will make its next rates decision on March 17. (Getty)

"ANZ has jumped the gun on the RBA, lifting fixed rates just four days out from the central bank's next decision," Canstar.com.au data insights director Sally Tindall said.

"Fixed rates are typically the early warning signal for where rates are headed. When they start creeping up before an RBA meeting it's a sign lenders are pricing in a hike before it materialises.

"The fact that the majority of ANZ's fixed rates now sit above 6 per cent will feel like a psychological shift for borrowers."

A﻿NZ this week also followed the other big four banks in predicting triple rate hike pain for Aussie borrowers.

CBA, Westpac and NAB yesterday shifted their cash rate predictions due to inflation running too hot, a stretched labour market, and the war in the Middle East – which is likely to add to inflation.

The impact on borrowers if the cash rate is to increase next Tuesday. (Nine)

All four big banks now anticipate the Reserve Bank will increase the cash rate 25 basis points when it meets next Tuesday, and by the same margin at the following meeting in May.

The RBA increased the interest rate by 0.25 per cent at its most recent meeting on February 3,﻿ to 3.85 per cent.

A triple hike would bring the cash rate to 4.35 per cent.﻿

title: Iconic Aussie music festival suddenly cancelled at short notice
text:
Iconic and long-running Byron Bay music festival Bluesfest has been suddenly cancelled weeks before it was due to take place.

Festival organisers confirmed the festival will not go ahead in 2026 due to rising costs and the decline of the Australian live music scene.

"After 36 years as Australia's most awarded festival, we are deeply sorry to share that Bluesfest will not proceed in 2026," organisers said in a statement on social media.

Bluesfest has reportedly been cancelled just weeks before it was due to begin. (Bluesfest)

"This is an incredibly difficult decision, and we are so proud of everything Bluesfest has achieved: the music, the artists, the busking competition, our dedicated staff and volunteers, and the community that has grown around the festival over the past three decades."

Organisers blamed the rising cost of production, insurance, logistics and touring for the cancellation, adding that it is "no longer possible" to put on the event to the standard expected by ticketholders.﻿

A liquidator has been appointed to manage the festival's financial matters.

"Ticket holders, including parking pass customers and campers, will be contacted directly with information about the claims process and any potential refund arrangements," the statement continued.

"To our fans, artists, partners and the Byron Bay community, thank you for 36 unforgettable years.

"We are incredibly grateful to everyone who has been part of the Bluesfest journey."

READ MORE: Search underway for man who vanished off a houseboat amid Queensland floods

The festival was set to run over the Easter long weekend from April 2﻿-5 at Byron Events Farm in the coastal NSW town.

Around 100,000 fans attend the annual music event.﻿

Split Enz, Parkway Drive, Sublime, The Pogues and Counting Crows were among the biggest acts due to perform at this year's festival.﻿

Byron Shire Mayor Sarah Ndiaye said it was "sad news" for the local community.﻿

"Obviously a huge economic loss but also such a big part of the fabric of our community," she told the ABC.

"Anyone that's trying to arrange things that involve lots of international travel, with prices going up the way they are and the instability.

"I can only imagine it would be very difficult."

More to come.﻿

title: Qantas agrees to $105 million settlement over COVID flight credits
text:
Q﻿antas has agreed to a $105 million settlement after a class action was brought against the airline over its controversial COVID-19 flight credits.

The sum, which is subject to approval by the Federal Court of Australia, comes with what the airline says is "no admission of liability".

﻿The class action related to flights scheduled to depart between January 1, 2020, and November 1, 2022, that were cancelled by Qantas, and included allegations the airline breached its contractual obligations regarding refunds.

LIVE UPDATES: US refuelling plane crashes over Iraq

Q﻿antas has agreed to a $105 million settlement after a class action was brought against the airline over its controversial COVID-19 flight credits. (Supplied)

Andrew Paull, a partner at Echo Law, said today's decision would put compensation into the hands of hundreds of thousands of Qantas customers.

Echo Law﻿ commenced the class action against Qantas in August 2023.

"This class action was based on allegations that Qantas customers were contractually entitled to cash refunds when their flights were cancelled due to COVID travel restrictions," Paull said.

People wearing facemasks on a Qantas flight in 2020, during the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic. (Supplied)

"Instead of those refunds, Qantas customers were issued with restricted flight credits.

"The settlement sum is in addition to Qantas' public commitment, given shortly after the class action was commenced, to provide refunds to all COVID credit holders.

"Under the settlement, eligible customers will now be able to seek compensation in addition to any existing refund rights."

Once approved by the Federal Court, Echo Law and Qantas will notify affected customers of the settlement and their rights, including how to claim their share of the compensation.﻿

In a statement, Qantas﻿ said it had made provisions for a potential payout, which would be reflected in future earnings reports.

"An increase reflecting the settlement will be recognised outside of underlying earnings in the second half of FY26," the statement said.

title: Georgie Gardner calls time after more than two decades at Nine
text:
Respected journalist and presenter Georgie Gardner has resigned from her role after a near 25-year career at Nine.

The Sydney newsreader, who will present her final 9News Sydney bulletin on Friday, April 17, said it was time for fresh beginnings.

"Time now to embark on a new chapter and not yet sure what that looks like, but I'm open to new opportunities and I'm excited," she said.

Respected journalist and presenter Georgie Gardner said it was time for "fresh beginnings". (Instagram)

"So much still to learn and discover in the world, and at age 55, so much still to contribute.

"Starting out at 2NX Newcastle in 1992 as a wide-eyed 21-yr-old from Perth, little did I realise my career path would be so rich and rewarding.

"Opening my mind to new ideas, satiating my curiosity for news and current affairs, highlighting causes for positive change, and accommodating my passion for communicating and connecting with people… broadcasting has been a great gift.

"And then there are the countless colleagues who've enriched my journey, along with all those I've interviewed.

"They are the reason why the decision to resign has not been made lightly.

"So to them and our viewers, I am immensely grateful.

"It's been awesome, and it's time."

More to come...﻿

title: The four-letter acronym that could pull Trump out of Iran
text:
In the past year, some of the biggest swings on the financial markets can be directly attributed to one man: Donald Trump .

The sharpest drop came from his tariff announcement on his so-called Liberation Day .

The sharpest jump came several days later when he backed down.

READ MORE: Trump denies knowledge of report blaming US for school bombing ﻿

Donald Trump has a history of chickening out of unpopular decisions. (AP)

And a few days later, the market tanked again when he announced new tariffs.

For traders around the world, including in Australia , so much of their financial future is tethered to the unpredictable nature of the president.

And the results are far more severe this time around than in Trump's first term.

"In Trump's first term, there were two things that held him back," independent economist Saul Eslake told nine.com.au .

"One was that he didn't expect to win, and so he didn't really have a plan.

"And second, there were adults in the room, both on his staff and in the cabinet, to restrain his worst instincts."

His volatility has also rattled the traditional safe haven of investors: the US dollar .

"When markets feel very uncertain about the economic or geopolitical outlook, the US dollar goes up and US bond yields go down," Eslake said.

"Well, not any more."

Now, that safe-haven status has switched to the Swiss franc and to precious metals like gold and silver.

Wall Street has been in turmoil since Donald Trump went to war with Iran. (AP)

But traders have taken solace in a four-letter word: TACO .

The acronym, short for Trump Always Chickens Out , is based on the idea that the president will back down from his worst impulses once he sees the stock market response.

"Donald Trump tends to see the share markets as a barometer of his success," AMP economist My Bui said.

"Donald Trump actually backed out last year to appease the voters a little bit by getting rid of tariffs on furniture and cocoa, beef and bananas."

But said traders were buying and selling on the assumption Trump would back out of his war in Iran.

When markets opened after Trump began his war in Iran , it was a bloodbath.

The ASX lost $90 billion in a day . Wall Street lost $1.1 trillion.

Trump has been sending mixed messages in the meantime on the duration of the war, claiming simultaneously it was nearly over or with much worse to come.

A Thai cargo vessel on fire in the Straits of Hormuz. (AP)

But this war with Iran does not give him an easy option to "chicken out".

"He has always had an off-ramp," Eslake said.

"And most of the time when there's been an adverse market reaction to something stupid he has said or done, he takes the off-ramp.

"But it's not obvious that there's an off-ramp here."

Peace with Iran appears a difficult proposition. The US attacked the country during its negotiations, which should make it less likely to willingly enter ceasefire talks.

And the new supreme leader may be unwilling to back down after air strikes killed his father, mother, wife, sister and son.

For the US, simply pulling out of the region won't guarantee the Iranian regime won't use its weapons to stop shipping traffic in the Straits of Hormuz.

READ MORE: Epstein files involving uncorroborated claim about Trump published ﻿

title: Australia's No.1 retailer named as last year's winner plunges
text:
In the battle for Aussie dollars, a new winner has been crowned.

Kmart was named the country's No.1 retailer at the Power Retail awards in Melbourne on Thursday night.

The shopping giant owned by Wesfarmers — a Perth-based conglomerate valued at $85 billion — beat out Big W for the top spot.

Melbourne, Australia - January 2, 2015: Kmart store in Burwood in suburban Melbourne. Kmart is a discount department store owned by Wesfarmers. (Getty)

Big W, which is owned by $43 billion Woolworths Group, was last year's winner.

Power Retail's top Australian retailers, in order, are:

Kmart Myer Bunnings Warehouse Officeworks Chemist Warehouse Big W JB Hi-Fi The Iconic Dan Murphy's Adore Beauty BCF Rebel The Good Guys David Jones Petbarn Appliances Online Target Bonds Ikea

After winning last year, Big W tumbled down to No.6 on the list. (9Honey)

Power Retail, an industry outlet providing companies with news, data and insights, said its rankings were weighed according to four pillars: acquisition, conversion, fulfilment and retention.

Power Retail head of data David Fear said Kmart ticked all the boxes to reclaim top spot.

"We score retailers across five pillars, with the highest weighting on how well they integrate their online and in-store operations," he said.

"Kmart led the Index because they delivered where it matters most — and where most retailers still have gaps.

"Less than half show real-time store stock online. Only around 30 per cent have a dedicated app or wish list functionality. Kmart has all of them, backed by a strong returns policy and a cross-channel loyalty program.

"In a market where shoppers are rapidly changing their habits, that kind of completeness across the entire customer journey is what separates the leaders."

title: Jessica's family trip was ruined over a 'distressing' citizenship revelation
text:
Mass confusion over the United Kingdom's new Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) scheme and its impact on Australians with British ancestry has forced some Australian travellers to cancel trips abroad.

Dual Australian-British citizens must now travel on their UK passport to visit England, Scotland, Northern Ireland and Wales after the government updated its entry requirements.

The controversial new rules also means many Australians may unknowingly be a citizen of the UK if their parents were born in there or in a British territory.

Darwin couple J﻿essica and Steven Sangster's long-awaited family holiday to visit elderly parents in Scotland was ruined over this murky rule change.

Jessica Sangster (pictured) and her husband Steven cancelled a family trip to Scotland over the confusion. (Supplied)

Do you have a story? Contact reporter April Glover at april.glover@nine.com.au

Steven was born in Australia in 1977, but his father was born in Scotland and returned there to live, making Steven an automatic British citizen by descent.

The family had saved for years to visit his elderly and unwell parents this month, but were forced to cancel over the "messy" passport guidelines.

"It's been a mess, very stressful and distressing," Jessica told Nine.com.au.

"Steven has been back to the UK as a kid to visit family, but has never held his own UK passport.

"He's always travelled on his Australian passport."

The Sangsters had booked their tickets, applied for their ETAs and were ready to fly this month when they began reading about the impact on dual citizens and those with automatic British citizenship.

After it dawned on them that Steven may be an automatic British citizen, the couple attempted to contact UK authorities for clarity.

Every time, they were simply referred to the gov.uk website.

The UK government has guidelines on its website for how to check if you are British citizen.

It states that you may already be a citizen if you were born outside of the UK to a British father before January 1, 1983.

Steven falls into this category.﻿

"But there's no specific or individualised scenario information given. They don't give that advice at all," Jessica said.

A UK Home Office spokesperson previously confirmed that automatic citizenship does not need to be "claimed". (Getty)

A UK Home Office spokesperson previously confirmed that automatic citizenship does not need to be "claimed", provided conditions are met at birth.

"Since British citizens cannot use an ETA, dual nationals, including children who hold dual citizenship by descent, but who may never have held a British passport, must now travel using a valid UK passport or a certificate of entitlement," the spokesperson said.

"This requirement reflects the UK's updated border processes rather than a change in their citizenship status."

Australia's Smartraveller advice also notes that "British citizenship by descent is automatic if you meet the conditions".

"This means you don't need to apply for citizenship to be considered a citizen," the advice reads.

Plenty of trips back home to the UK hang in the balance over this murky rule. (AP)

As Steven has never had a valid British passport, the loophole allowing some travellers to board flights with an expired passport won't help him.﻿

With their flight to the UK nearing and no definitive answers, they made the tough decision to cancel their trip altogether.

The next step is trying to apply for a British passport, which Jessica described as a "logistical nightmare".

"We came to the conclusion that the turnaround of trying to get all the required information was not going to happen," she said.

"With his parents nearing 80, the requirement to get the UK passport is that they need to send their original birth certificate and marriage certificate.

"So they're very nervous to send that all the way to us in Australia."

Steven among tens of thousands of Australians who have been left ﻿in the dark over their presumed citizenship status in the UK.

The couple are asking the very same question many dual citizens have raised.

"Why has it been forced on us?" she said.

title: Western Australia the big GST winner again despite massive budget surpluses
text:
The West Australian government will get an extra $6.6 billion from Australian taxpayers in the coming financial year under its special GST deal, despite being the only state with a budget surplus.

The state will receive $9.3 billion from the GST in the distribution for 2026-27, which was published by the independent Commonwealth Grants Commission on Friday.

That is much more than the $2.7 billion it would have received if it followed the same rules as every other state and territory, where the GST is distributed based on an assessment of their capacity to raise their own money, and on their population's needs.

Western Australia, whose premiers have long complained the system was unfair because it penalised the state for its mineral wealth, struck a deal with the Morrison government in 2018 to guarantee a minimum share of the pie.

The deal was extended by the Albanese government, and sees the federal government pay extra money out of its non-GST revenue every year to ensure the other jurisdictions do not lose out under the new arrangements.

Economist Saul Eslake, a vociferous critic of the deal, told the ABC the deal had cost taxpayers $36 billion since then and would cost roughly $60 billion in the next five years.

"Ultimately the residents of Australia's richest state will come to enjoy better public services and lower state taxes than other Australians," he said.

"How Jim Chalmers and Anthony Albanese can reconcile that with their professed commitments to equity and getting the budget into 'better nick', as Chalmers likes to say, is beyond me."

Nobody goes backwards thanks to higher tax take

The biggest loser of this year's distribution was New South Wales, which saw its share fall from 26.7 per cent of the total to 25.5 per cent. Apart from Western Australia, which went from 8.3 per cent to 9.1 per cent, the biggest winner was Queensland, which went from 17.4 per cent to 18 per cent.

The main reason for the NSW decline was a revised estimate of their natural disaster relief expenses, less than previously expected. The main reason for the Queensland gain was a fall in mining royalty revenue.

But every state and territory will receive more overall from the GST in 2026-27 than in 2025-26, because the total GST collected has increased by nearly $6 billion.

For NSW, the gain is a modest $316 million. Queensland's gain is $1.7 billion and Victoria's $1.5 billion.

These redistributions are technically a proposal that the federal treasurer must approve, but this is generally a formality.

How the GST is distributed

The federal government collects the GST, but has an arrangement with states and territories that sees all the revenue transferred to them. This is not the only federal money states and territories receive, but is a significant portion of it.

Rather than split it up "evenly" per person, the federal government has opted for a system that is scaled according to need and capacity, based on the principle that every Australian should expect a similar standard of frontline services.

The result is that states assessed as "high need" get much more than what they would get under a population share. This is measured using "relativity" scores, where a score of one is the population share, higher scores are more, and lower scores are less.

The Northern Territory, which has the highest level of need, has a score of 5.24 for 2026-27, meaning it gets more than five times its population share.

Tasmania (1.89), South Australia (1.35), the Australian Capital Territory (1.16) and Victoria (1.06) will receive higher than their population share. New South Wales (0.82) and Queensland (0.87) will receive less.

Western Australia would have had a score of just 0.24 under the same system, meaning it would get less than a quarter of its population share, mostly reflecting its very high capacity to collect mining royalties.

But the deal ensures it cannot receive less than the lowest of Victoria and New South Wales, and can never receive less than 0.75. This year, that system has resulted in the same 0.82 score as New South Wales.

The GST distribution does not take into account the actual revenue and spending of states and territories, but rather their potential to raise revenue and their need to spend. This means states are not "rewarded" for being in debt or "punished" for surpluses.

The West Australian government ran a budget surplus of $2.5 billion in 2024-25 and projects surpluses every year until 2028-29.

The state's deal is due to expire in 2029, but the WA government has been lobbying for its extension. In a national ad campaign titled "Keep the GST Fair", the government argues that the extra GST helps it to "support the national economy" with its exports.

title: Illegal tobacco retailer's $1.5m fine 'excessive', court finds
text:
One of Australia's biggest illegal tobacco and vape fines has been drastically cut.

The penalty against Tobacco Shed Pty Ltd, which had a string of stores busted selling illegal goods, was cut from $1.49 million to $250,000 after a legal appeal.

The Emerald District Court ruled the initial fine — far beyond what prosecutors had even sought — was excessive and errors occurred in sentencing.

The appeal marks the latest tussle involving the ballooning illicit tobacco trade and authorities.

The Tobacco Shed at Blackwater was one of the stores raided in 2023. (Supplied: Queensland Health)

Five Tobacco Shed stores were raided in August 2023 in Emerald, Rockhampton, Blackwater and Yeppoon, in central Queensland. Seven months later, another 158 vapes were seized from another Tobacco Shed store at the Gold Coast's Benowa.

Altogether more than 213,000 cigarettes (almost 10,600 packs), 25 kilograms of "chop chop" raw tobacco and 6,600 vapes were taken.

Illegal tobacco retailer hit with $1.5m fine following Queensland sting A Victorian retailer has been fined $1.5 million following a court case over Queensland's largest-ever illegal tobacco seizure.

The ABC earlier revealed the sole director of Tobacco Shed at the time of the first raids was Sam Kanjo, a man who has had multiple brushes with authorities and pleaded guilty to a nicotine vape selling charge in NSW in 2022.

Mr Kanjo told the ABC that while he was the company's director, he was not in control of the Queensland stores when the first raids took place and he was not involved in the illicit trade there. He said he was no longer involved in the tobacco trade and had moved overseas, and others took over the directorship from early 2024.

Gold Coast-based businessman Sam Kanjo was previously sole director of Tobacco Shed Pty Ltd. (Supplied: Linkedin)

Prosecutors in November last year in the Emerald Magistrates Court had sought a fine of between $200,000 and $300,000 after Tobacco Shed pleaded guilty to 50 offences, including breaches of tobacco sales laws.

Magistrate Cameron McKenzie at the time said he was "nowhere within the realms of that" fine proposal, and noted penalties in other courts had hardly deterred the trade.

In a ruling published late Thursday, District Court Judge Jeffrey Clarke said the offending was serious, but the initial decision impermissibly used a mathematical calculation of 15 per cent of the theoretical maximum of a $9.95 million, and courts must not take a "staged approach" to calculating penalties.

The biggest unpaid debt from illegal tobacco dealers revealed Illicit tobacco retailers have racked up millions in unpaid fines since the introduction of stronger penalties, with one owing more than $7 million.

He wrote that, among other problems, imposing a fine five times higher than that contended for by the prosecution and agreed to by defence lawyers then "did not account for the fact that much of the offending had happened at the same time, across the different stores".

The fine remains at the higher end of penalties imposed against retailers for selling illicit tobacco and vapes. Of the Australian agencies overseeing tobacco surveyed by the ABC earlier this year, NSW had one at $288,700 imposed last year.

Recouping fines themselves have been a problem, with tens of millions of dollars in penalties going unpaid in Queensland alone.

title: Outback pregnancy-to-parenting service calls for funding
text:
A support organisation helping new families navigate pregnancy and early parenthood in Alice Springs will have to close its doors without an urgent injection of funding, according to its board.

Birth and Beyond is a long-running not-for-profit community organisation that parents say fills a critical gap in services available to new and expecting parents in Central Australia.

Hannah Blaine says Birth and Beyond is important because it takes a village to raise children. (ABC News: William Green)

Organisation chair Hannah Blaine, a paediatric clinical neuropsychologist in town, said the recent loss of their only staff member, in part due to workload and funding constraints, highlighted the need for ongoing government support.

Funding falling short

From birth classes, library resources and equipment hire to social gatherings, workshops and a place to drop in with a baby to take a beat in the centre of town, Ms Blaine said Birth and Beyond offered vital non-clinical support.

"If we just look at the 2024-25 financial year, [we did] over 150 referrals out to other providers and services in town that families needed to access," Ms Blaine said.

Hannah Blaine says they need funding from Northern Territory and federal governments to operate. (ABC News: Elsie Lange)

The organisation receives funding from the Northern Territory health department, which is due to wrap up in 2027, but Ms Blaine said it was not enough to keep providing their current services, as rent, electricity, insurance and wage costs continued to rise.

Seventy per cent of their services, Ms Blaine said, were currently unfunded and required volunteer time, which was not sustainable.

"We want to keep providing these services to the community, but without funding to do that, we end up with the situation we're in at the moment, where we've had to close our doors," she said.

Birth and Beyond has received nearly 240 drop-ins for support in 2024-25. (ABC News: Elsie Lange)

Ms Blaine said the organisation had approached both the NT and federal governments about funding options, but had yet to hear anything confirmed.

The federal member for Lingiari, Marion Scrymgour, said the Commonwealth provided $3.5 billion in health funding to the NT and that it was "appalling" the NT government was not spending it on making Birth and Beyond's operations sustainable.

NT Health minister Steve Edgington said his department had been underfunded for years by the Commonwealth, but that discussions with Birth and Beyond for further funding remained "ongoing".

Parents say having a welcoming place in town to take a breather with their children has been invaluable. (ABC News: Elsie Lange)

Balm for isolated for parents

Local counsellor and mum, Hannah Muir, hosts perinatal mental health workshops through Birth and Beyond and said the service was empowering.

Hannah Muir says Birth and Beyond is a safe and welcoming space for the families in town. (ABC News: William Green)

"It is so incredible … it can be even more isolating having a baby in Alice Springs because a lot of people are away from their usual support systems," Ms Muir said.

"[Motherhood is] really hard and it's really gritty and it's really messy.

"Not having a space like this … does just mean that we'll have more women, more mothers, more people with small children [feeling] shrouded in secrecy and isolation and, I think in terms of mental health, that's really damaging."

Viv Andersen says the organisation fills a vital gap in support services in Alice Springs. (ABC News: William Green)

Midwife Viv Andersen said while the public health system in Alice Springs was excellent, publicly available antenatal classes quickly filled up, so classes at Birth and Beyond were a great added resource.

"It's been able to do so much for us, for community, for getting out of the house, for giving information, for bringing in other new parents that are at a loose end with what to do with their days because it can be a bit monotonous at times," Ms Andersen said.

Shay Cornell says Birth and Beyond connected her with other mums expecting twins. (ABC News: William Green)

When she was pregnant with her three-year-old twins, Shay Cornell said having in-person workshops and classes had been important during the pregnancy journey and afterwards.

"There's nothing else like this in town, but I think what we also don't realise is that other places around Australia do have these services," Ms Cornell said.

Birth and Beyond's funding from the NT government is due to finish in 2027. (ABC News: Elsie Lange)

Ms Blaine said the space that Birth and Beyond created for new parents was unique.

"Sometimes you do just wake up in that early postpartum [time] and you just things aren't feeling right," Ms Blaine said.

"It's not [a] crisis, and it's not something that you need to access a GP or a child health nurse about.

" But it's really powerful and healing to come in and just sit with someone who's able to listen and not necessarily offer advice, but just to say you're doing a good job. "

title: Areas most at risk of 'devastating' cattle disease incursion revealed
text:
Modelling suggests Queensland's northern tip is the most likely entry point for a potentially devastating cattle disease to be carried into Australia by wind.

The research from the University of Queensland (UQ) has also identified Port Hedland in Western Australia's Pilbara region as another of the most likely locations for lumpy skin disease virus to enter the country.

The disease, which has spread throughout Africa, the Middle East, southern Europe and into South-East Asia, could wipe $7 billion from Australia's farming sector in its first year, according to analysis.

The virus, which is spread by mosquitoes, flies and ticks, can cause lesions, fever and weight loss in cattle. It poses a major threat to Australia's multi-billion-dollar beef and dairy industries, but experts say its risk of incursion remains low.

The map shows the most likely entry points for lump skin disease in Australia, with Cape York and Port Hedland showing the highest risk. (Supplied: Univeristy of Queensland)

However, the research, published in Nature's Scientific Reports, highlights which areas are at a higher risk and could aid in surveillance, according to one of its authors, Ricardo Soares Magalhães.

"These maps can pinpoint locations that in particular times of the year could be intensified for vector surveillance," Professor Soares Magalhães said.

He said the Cape York Peninsula has been flagged as a very high-risk area for windborne arrival, because the virus could be carried into Australia via insects on strong winds, particularly during the summer months.

Cape York Peninsula is the region most likely to carry lumpy skin disease into Australia through wind. (Supplied: University of Queensland)

In Western Australia, Professor Soares Magalhães said the risk is due to shipping activity — with a very high risk in Port Hedland and a moderate one in Dampier — where this virus could hitchhike its way onto shore.

"This is an important finding because Port Hedland is a major entry point in terms of trade volume," he said.

"That could be another opportunity to focus the attention to that area there to monitor the mosquito circulation, the vector circulation in those locations."

The map shows which areas lumpy skin disease is more likely to enter Australia through shipping ports. (Supplied: Univeristy of Queensland)

The wind risk is also moderate to high at the top of the Northern Territory.

Professor Soares Magalhães said there was no need to worry about an incursion.

"We've got ample surveillance happening across the board," he said.

This map shows the high-risk entry points for lumpy skin disease to enter Australia via strong winds. (Supplied: University of Queensland)

Greg Pankhurst of the Australian Livestock Exporters' Council said he welcomed the research and said it was important to be aware of the risks.

He said it would be devastating if the virus made it to Australian shores, and the research provided information to improve surveillance.

"I think this initiative by UQ is imperative for the industry to get a little bit of an idea of where the disease is, and let's hope it never does, if it does cross our shores," Mr Pankhurst said.

Lumpy skin disease has been in Indonesia since 2022. (Supplied: FAO/Eko Prianto)

Mr Pankhurst is also a consultant with Lampung Livestock, which operates in Indonesia.

He said more work needs to be done to limit the spread of the disease, first detected in Australia's northern neighbour in 2022.

"I still spend a lot of time in Indonesia. I was in Indonesia last week, and I saw lumpy skin disease," he said.

"Wouldn't it be good to make sure it doesn't come ashore? And the only way we can do that is to slow it down or help the Indonesians eradicate it on their side," he said.

title: Controversy at Women's Asian Cup after former coach leads 'Taiwan' chant
text:
The Women's Asian Cup has been embroiled in another diplomatic controversy, with Taiwanese officials lodging a complaint after a former national coach was ejected from a stadium for leading a chant that included the word "Taiwan."

The incident has raised political tensions ahead of tomorrow's high profile quarter-final clash between China and Chinese Taipei — the name Taiwan competes under due to Asian Football Confederation rules.

Social media videos show Chen Kuei-jen orchestrating fellow supporters in a chant of "Taiwan: add oil!" — a Chinese language phrase which loosely translates to "let's go Taiwan!" — during its group stage match against India at Parramatta Stadium this week.

The incident has raised tensions ahead of the quarter-final clash between China and Chinese Taipei — the name Taiwan competes under. (Supplied: Lala Kao)

Security officers repeatedly approached Chen during the first half, and during the half time break they told him he wasn't allowed to lead "political" chants, before escorting him from the stadium.

Video captured by another fan shows the security guard saying he was only following directions from the Asian Football Confederation, while Taiwanese fans complain they are being singled out and treated unfairly.

"This is what I've been told," the guard says.

"If you want to take it up with the AFC later, you can."

The move has frustrated several fans of Taiwan's team and the head of Taiwan's de facto embassy in Canberra, Douglas Hsu, has now written to the Asian Football Confederation to complain about "double standards" imposed on their supporters.

"We strongly believe that sporting events should not become politicised spaces where certain fans are treated differently based on identity or expression of support for their team," he said.

The move has frustrated several fans of Taiwan's team. (Supplied: Lala Kao)

"To uphold the integrity and credibility of sport, we urge the AFC to remain its independence and neutrality from any political, legal, or economic pressures, and expect to apply equal and fair treatment for all spectators, no matter their races, religions, nationalities, or political orientations, in the upcoming matches, like the one between Chinese Taipei vs China."

Many international sporting bodies restrict the use of Taiwan's flag and require the team to compete as "Chinese Taipei", a compromise adopted after the International Olympic Committee recognised the People's Republic of China in the late 1970s.

For example at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris, during a badminton match between Taiwanese players and Denmark, several Taiwanese spectators said venue staff asked them to remove towels or banners displaying the word "Taiwan" while cheering for the athletes.

A member of security asks a supporter holding a banner in reference to Taiwan to leave the stand during a badminton match at the Paris 2024 Olympics. (Reuters: Ann Wang)

But that approach isn't universal.

For example, earlier this month thousands of Taiwanese fans gathered outside the Tokyo Dome in Japan ahead of the opening game of the World Baseball Classic against Australia, waving banners reading "Team Taiwan" and chanting "Go Taiwan" in support of the team.

Taiwan soccer fans in Australia told the ABC they felt "suppressed" by seemingly arbitrary and heavy-handed rules imposed by the AFC, despite their efforts to keep within regulations by not bringing any Taiwan flags to display.

Thousands of Taiwanese fans gathered outside the Tokyo Dome in Japan ahead of the opening game of the World Baseball Classic against Australia. (Reuters: Issei Kato)

One supporter, Lala Kao, told the ABC they "simply want to cheer for our team".

"There is no clear rule in the stadium saying you cannot say: "Go Taiwan." So there is one question in my mind: is it wrong for me to say I am Taiwanese?" she said.

"Is even saying 'Go Taiwan' not allowed?

"We have always followed the rules and have not done anything that violates them. Yet it feels like we are constantly being suppressed."

She said she was also banned from bringing in a sign to an earlier game between Taiwan and Vietnam, even though it kept strictly to the rules.

"The sign only had the Chinese Taipei Football Association logo, the players' numbers, their Chinese names, and the emblem of the Chinese Taipei Olympic Committee," she said.

"The staff suddenly told me the sign could not be brought into the stadium, but they did not give me a specific reason … they told me either to lock the sign away or not enter the stadium.

" I was very upset, because I had already brought it in on Wednesday, and there was nothing on the sign that violated any rules. "

The ABC has approached the AFC for comment, but it has not yet responded.

A spokesperson for the Asian Cup local organising committee said the incident was still being investigated.

The ABC has also approached China's Embassy in Canberra for comment.

title: Byron Bay Bluesfest cancelled at short notice
text:
Byron Bay's famous Bluesfest has been cancelled, less than three weeks before it was due to begin.

In a statement released online, organisers said they were "deeply sorry" to share the news.

"This is an incredibly difficult decision, and we are so proud of everything Bluesfest has achieved ... and the community that has grown around the festival over the past three decades," they said.

"Rising production, logistics, insurance and touring costs, combined with a challenging environment for major live events, mean it is no longer possible to deliver the festival to the standard our audiences, artists and partners expect."

The statement said a liquidator had been appointed to manage all financial matters, and ticketholders would be contacted directly with information about the claims process and any potential refund arrangements.

Byron Bay's Bluesfest was due to start on April 2 but has been cancelled. (Supplied: Dave kan) ]

The music festival, which began in 1990, has played host to some of the world's biggest stars including Bob Dylan, Midnight Oil and R.E.M.

This year's event was scheduled to run from April 2–5, with Split Enz and local hardcore heroes Parkway Drive the headline acts.

Split Enz were due to kick off their Australian tour at Bluesfest before playing arenas in May. (Supplied: Live Nation)

More than 100,000 people passed though the gates last year, but some left angry at Bluesfest founder Peter Noble after he backflipped on his announcement that 2025 would be the festival's final year.

The event was also hit hard by the COVID-19 pandemic, with the festivals planned for 2020 and 2021 called off at short notice by the NSW government.

Peter Noble had marketed the 2025 Bluesfest as the last. (Supplied: Bluesfest)

The cancellation of Bluesfest will be a blow to the local economy, with a report commissioned by organisers estimating the 2024 event contributed more than $230 million to the NSW economy.

Byron Shire Mayor Sarah Ndiaye said it was "very sad news" for the community.

"Obviously a huge economic loss but also such a big part of the fabric of our community," she said.

"Very much feeling for all the people involved, obviously very difficult times with all the things that are going on in the world at the moment.

"Anyone that's trying to arrange things that involve lots of international travel, with prices going up the way they are and the instability … I can only imagine it would be very difficult."

Ash Grunwald played Bluesfest 11 times. (Jeremy Story Carter, ABC)

Australian blues musician Ash Grunwald, who has played at the festival 11 times, described the news as "devastating".

"I feel really, really saddened, it was an amazing festival," he said.

"It gave [musicians] exposure to all of Australia because everybody went to it, and also because they had amazing international acts, which as a musician was very inspiring to see.

"The festival has just been an amazing thing for your whole career, and it was for mine, and it really helped to make me."

title: An agreement has been reached on Qantas COVID flight credits
text:
Qantas has agreed to settle a class action related to flight credits issued during the COVID-19 ‌pandemic.

The settlement, subject to approval by the Federal Court, relates to flights scheduled to depart between January 2020 and November 2022 that were ⁠cancelled by the airline.

Filed in August 2023, the class action included allegations that Qantas breached contractual ⁠obligations by failing to provide refunds for cancelled flights during the pandemic.

Qantas has agreed to settle the class action for $105 million, with no admission of liability.

Qantas agrees to settle the class action for $105 million, with no admission of liability. (AP: Mark Baker)

The settlement is higher than the $55 million provision that Qantas Airways mentioned in its latest half-year results in February for the flight credits class action.

Qantas prepared for financial hit

Analysts said Qantas delayed the inevitable by engaging in a protracted legal case.

"When a flight is cancelled, the law generally requires a refund," The Montgomery Fund founder Roger Montgomery said.

Alan Joyce was Qantas CEO until 2023. (ABC News: John Gunn)

At the time Qantas chief executive Alan Joyce said Qantas customers could request a cash refund for their Qantas COVID travel credits at "any point in the future".

"These credits and vouchers will never expire," Mr Joyce said in a video statement.

"We're doing this because we've listened.

"We know the credit system was not as smooth as it should have been.

"And while we've improved it recently, and extended the expiry date several times, people lost faith in the process."

Mr Montgomery said this Qantas response was not received well by Qantas shareholders.

"Making credits the default and adding expiry dates to them made Qantas look dodgy and put them on shaky legal ground," he said.

Qantas has committed to 10 per cent sustainable aviation fuel starting from 2030. (ABC News: Keana Naughton)

Despite this, Mr Montgomery said, investors would take very little notice of this legal news.

"The airline had already provisioned for the legal costs and probably already expected to pay, so this settlement won't hurt financially, and shareholders are content because the 'uncertainty' is kicked into touch," he said.

Mr Montgomery says the airline may hope this settlement, if approved by the Federal Court, will assist with its turnaround strategy under new CEO Vanessa Hudson.

Alan Joyce collects $3.8 million in final Qantas payout The national carrier's annual report shows Alan Joyce will pick up his final bonus — a stash of shares worth millions — on Friday's market.

"Qantas likely realised a courtroom loss would be far more expensive and damaging than a $105 million payout," he said.

"Don't forget they already lost significant ground with court losses over selling tickets to ghost flights and illegal layoffs during the pandemic.

"[Qantas] just needs to move on and be a good corporate citizen for once.

"It also helps CEO Vanessa Hudson show the airline has moved past the Alan Joyce era."

Vanessa Hudson succeeded Joyce as CEO in September 2023. (ABC News: John Gunn)

Cash could hit bank accounts this year

Echo Law commenced the class action on behalf of Qantas customers who held tickets for domestic or international flights that were cancelled by Qantas between 2020 and 2022, including due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

"In the coming weeks the Court will be asked to approve a notice to Group Members confirming the details of the proposed settlement and setting out what Group Members must do to claim their share," the law firm said in a statement.

"Until that notice is distributed, there is nothing that Group Members need to do."

Global ranking reveals poor value for Aussie airline loyalty programs Millions of Australians are signed up to airline loyalty schemes, but a new global ranking finds travellers are getting poorer value than from overseas carriers.

The airline says the settlement amount will be paid to a court-approved administrator and affected customers can expect money in their bank account by the end of this year.

For Qantas customers, it added in a statement today, "a 'COVID Credit' refers to travel credits for bookings up to and including 31 September 2021."

title: The 37 seconds of India's celebration that highlight cricket's problem
text:
If you have spent any time on the internet this week following India's T20 World Cup win, you will have come across it.

What is "it" you ask? It is a 37-second video posted by India's social media team that is essentially a celebration montage from the final against New Zealand.

The 37-second clip is a super-cut of 16 different scenes mashed together. Of the 16 scenes, ICC chairman Jay Shah features in 12.

In the video, Shah congratulates Indian coach Gautam Gambhir in several scenes; in another, he is waving to the crowd in Narendra Modi Stadium while walking alongside Rohit Sharma, the skipper of the 2024 World Cup-winning Indian side.

There is even a solo shot of Shah holding the trophy, standing alone on the dais, as if he hit the winning runs in the final. He eventually lifts the trophy alongside Suryakumar Yadav, like he is India's co-captain.

Just 37 years old, Shah is cricket's youngest ever boss. He previously held roles as the secretary of the BCCI (India's cricket board) and was also the president of the Asian Cricket Council (ACC). He is the son of India's Home Affairs Minister Amit Shah.

Shah is the third Indian to be chairman since the ICC revamped its management structure in 2014.

Shah (right) pictured with then-Indian captain Rohit Sharma (left) after India's Champions Trophy win last year. (Getty Images: Matthew Lewis)

The Jay Shah sizzle reels, as they have been called in some corners of the internet, have now become commonplace after ICC tournaments since Shah began his tenure in December 2024.

Shah outshone South African skipper Temba Bavuma in an ICC video following the Proteas' Test Championship Final win over Australia last year, a video that was widely mocked online.

While the clip from the WTC Final was laughable, the ICC wanting to promote its boss made sense in a roundabout, twisted way, even if you would not see the boss of any other sport featured this much in a highlight clip from the final.

It is like Gianni Infantino featuring more than Lionel Messi in a highlight video from the 2022 FIFA World Cup final.

The boss of cricket featuring this much in a video posted by the BCCI and showing a copious amount of glee in India's win makes you wonder where the ICC's priorities truly lie.

There is no questioning that Shah has been good for Indian cricket. His major wins include the creation of the WPL, the biggest women's cricket league in the world, the successful hosting of the 2023 men's World Cup in India, and ensuring India's men's and women's players receive equal prize money from the ICC.

Shah has also been instrumental in cricket being included in the 2028 Olympics. However, beyond that, it is not clear how he has helped the game grow as a whole.

Shah has been instrumental in the rise of women's cricket in India, culminating in last year's World Cup win. (Getty Images: Alex Davidson)

It was fitting that the T20 World Cup basically started and ended with questions over India's considerable influence on the game, and whether it is abusing its power.

Bangladesh was booted out of the tournament in the lead-up for refusing to play matches in India due to security concerns, despite India having done the same with Pakistan for the Champions Trophy.

The Bangladesh Cricket Board (BCB) asked for all its matches to be played in Sri Lanka, the tournament's co-host, but India decided this was not good enough and sent the side packing, with Scotland taking its place.

This was followed by India's real scare of the tournament — Pakistan threatening to boycott its clash against India in solidarity with Bangladesh.

India and Pakistan eventually faced off at the T20 World Cup despite an earlier boycott threat. (Getty Images: Robert Cianflone)

No matter where a tournament is held, who the favourites are, or any other factors, you will find that India and Pakistan are always in the same pool at an ICC tournament.

The reason? The incredible interest in the fixture generates more money for the ICC and its members than anything else in world cricket, something to the tune of $US250 million in revenue.

The match was eventually played in Sri Lanka and was won by India, as usually tends to be the case at an ICC tournament.

India crossed the 200-run mark in just one of its first five matches of the World Cup. In the fifth of those matches, against South Africa, they were rolled for just 111 in Ahmedabad, the same venue where the final was played.

India was humbled by South Africa earlier in the World Cup before finishing the tournament in style. (Reuters: Amit Dave)

Following that 76-run loss, India scored more than 250 in three of its next four matches, scoring 199 in a run chase against the West Indies in the other. It must be said that on the three occasions India breached the 250 mark, it was asked to bat first by the opposing skipper each time.

Unlike bilateral series, where the home team gets to prepare whatever wickets it wants, ICC tournaments do not allow the surface to be controlled in such a way, even if you are the host nation.

Nevertheless, due to the common threads between the ICC, the BCCI and Shah himself, it opens India up to suggestions that it had asserted its power to alter the nature of pitches for the remainder of the World Cup.

This is sad on two counts. One, it takes the attention completely away from some absolutely sensational individual performances that delivered India the trophy.

Sanju Samson finished as the Player of the Tournament after three match-winning performances at the top of India's order. (Reuters: Sahiba Chawdhary)

Sanju Samson, a batter seemingly forever on the fringes of the Indian team, became a household name in not only Indian cricket but in world cricket.

Jasprit Bumrah cemented his status as one of the greatest bowlers to ever play the sport.

Secondly, the cloud of India having too much power in the game hovered over what was an incredibly entertaining tournament overall.

This was the second T20 World Cup to be expanded to 20 teams, and featured Italy for the first time.

Zimbabwe handsomely beat Australia in the group stage in one of the upsets of the World Cup. (Getty: Matthew Lewis/ICC)

For a sport that has been crying out for the other nations to challenge the "Big 3" of India, England and Australia, this tournament delivered in spades.

Zimbabwe knocked off both Australia and Sri Lanka to reach the Super 8s, the US had India reeling and won two matches, while both Italy and Nepal came within a few blows of beating England.

Canada's Yuvraj Samra provided one of the innings of the tournament when he smashed a 65-ball 110 against New Zealand, an innings that made him the first player from an associate nation to score a World Cup century.

The associate nations, even in their losses, showed that there is absolute value in choosing to invest in them. Whether the ICC does that or not is an entirely different question.

Pre-seeding meant the West Indies and Zimbabwe were both in the same Super 8s group despite both winning their initial groups. (Getty Images: Nikhil Patil)

After all of the excitement from a genuinely entertaining group stage, in classic ICC style, the tournament scored an own goal in a way only cricket can by going with pre-seeding for the Super 8s, where the groups were decided not by the performances in the tournament, but each team's ICC world ranking in T20s.

It left us with a farcical situation where each of the group winners were placed in one pool while each of the losers were placed in the other.

Imagine a FIFA World Cup where France faces Argentina early on in the tournament simply because of their world ranking, rather than how they fared in the group stage.

It is these instances of shooting itself in the foot that truly hold cricket back in the eyes of many.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

This brings us all back to Shah, his sizzle reels and the objectives of his role as the chairman of the ICC.

When Shah was unanimously appointed to the role midway through 2024, he said: "My commitment is to work closely with the ICC team and our member nations to globalise cricket further."

Since he began in his role in December of that year, India has won every tournament that matters, while everyone else has been relegated to watching Shah lapping it up with the players and snatching the trophy from their hands at every opportunity.

Is Shah's remit to make cricket stronger as a whole, or to make India stronger at the expense of its rivals?

Indian fans will read all of this, say "cry more", and point to their trophy cabinet and their coffers as enough evidence that cricket is in a good spot.

The rest of the world might just beg to differ, particularly those countries that currently reside outside of the lavish "Big 3" bungalow.

title: FIFA, AFC accused of failing their human rights obligations with Iranian players
text:
The plight of the Iranian women's football team in Australia has exposed a gap between the words and actions of FIFA and the Asian Football Confederation (AFC) when it comes to human rights, according to some experts.

Neither football's governing body nor the organisers of the Women's Asian Cup could confirm if a human rights risk assessment took place before the tournament, despite requests from the global players' union FIFPRO.

And a growing chorus is urging the sport's powerbrokers to protect the members of the women's team currently making the journey home, while ensuring there is not a repeat of the situation at the upcoming men's World Cup.

Even before the US and Israeli strikes on Tehran occurring a day before the first Women's Asian Cup match, there were ongoing concerns for the safety of the travelling women's team after tens of thousands of protesters were killed in the months prior.

Iran's last appearance at a global football tournament, the 2022 men's World Cup in Qatar, was also marked by protests against the regime.

The Woman, Life, Freedom movement featured prominently in the wake of Mahsa Amini's death in the custody of the nation's so-called morality police, with the men's team in Qatar performing similar actions around the anthem — not singing before their first game — to the women's team at the Asian Cup.

The Woman, Life, Freedom protest movement arrived at the men's FIFA World Cup in 2022 after Mahsa Amini died in the custody of Iran's so-called morality police. (Getty Images: Robbie Jay Barratt/AMA)

Dr Leila Khanjaninejad, an Iranian-Australian gender equity researcher at the University of Technology Sydney, said "most of us knew" something similar could occur at the Asian Cup, and organisers should have been able to foresee it too.

"These governing bodies cannot wait for the crisis to happen and become public, then take action," Dr Khanjaninejad told ABC Sport.

"Even though there is a human rights commitment from FIFA and AFC on paper, we are seeing very explicitly that there is a gap between the commitments they have on paper and the protection they offer in practice to athletes.

Mother of Iranian footballer pleads for her to stay in Australia The smiles and joy seen in pictures of the seven members of the Iranian women's football team who sought asylum in Australia only tell part of the story, with many still scared for their families back home.

"Nobody is expecting them to resolve the geopolitics or the big issues, but these are very powerful organisations and they have the responsibility not to just run a tournament but to protect the people inside them."

Members of the Iranian-Australian community have started a petition, to ask FIFA to protect the players who are returning to Iran.

"After all, FIFA's role is to support footballers, and it needs to understand the situation our female football players are facing," Haleh Nazari, who is gathering signatures, told ABC News in Farsi.

"FIFA must know what conditions our women footballers are living under, and how they and their families may be at risk.

"We hope they can help ensure their safety.

"So far, FIFA has shown no [public] reaction to what has happened to the Iranian women’s national team, and we remain very concerned for the players' lives."

What are FIFA's human rights obligations?

In 2017, FIFA published what it described as a "landmark" human rights policy on the back of a report by Harvard professor John Ruggie.

It came after questions were raised about human rights abuses in the lead-up to the 2018 men's World Cup in Russia, and the awarding of the 2022 edition to Qatar.

The policy says FIFA adheres to the United Nations' Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, and "embraces all internationally recognised human rights", including the International Bill of Human Rights.

Seven members of the Iranian women's football team originally sought asylum in Australia. One has since decided to return to Iran. (X: Tony Burke)

There are a number of pillars governing this policy, including identifying and addressing "potential and actual adverse human rights impacts associated with its activities and address[ing] such risks through adequate prevention and mitigation measures".

A large part of that is due diligence to "continuously identify" any issues that may crop up in relation to a FIFA-sanctioned event.

The policy also stipulates regular and transparent communication from FIFA with stakeholders and the general public about its human rights efforts.

The AFC is one of FIFA's six continental confederations, which is then comprised of the 211 national associations.

FIFPRO says it raised concerns over Iranian players' safety with FIFA and the AFC in February.

FIFPRO Asia/Oceania president Beau Busch told ABC Sport there should have been "a comprehensive human rights impact assessment" carried out before the tournament.

"That would've identified the risk of political persecution to players potentially seeking asylum [and ensured] there was available support services for them to understand their rights in relation to that," he said.

Who are the Iranian players granted asylum? Australia initially granted five Iranian players asylum after their Women's Asian Cup campaign. So, who are they?

"We're not aware of that having occurred, we really believe that should have occurred and that certainly needs to occur going forward ahead of all major tournaments."

FIFA ordered the Australian Human Rights Commission (AHRC) and its New Zealand counterpart to conduct an independent assessment for the last global football tournament in Australia, the 2023 Women's World Cup, and it was handed down in 2021.

That report flagged the possibility of some members of visiting teams seeking asylum, based on similar instances at the 2000 Sydney Olympics and 2018 Gold Coast Commonwealth Games.

Within that, the report raised concerns about the potential for refoulement, when an asylum seeker or refugee is forced to return to a country where they are likely to face persecution.

Iran played three games on the Gold Coast at the Women's Asian Cup.

"The AHRC was not asked to conduct a similar assessment for the 2026 AFC Women's Asian Cup … [and] is not aware if a similar risk assessment was done," a spokesperson told the ABC.

ABC Sport has contacted FIFA and the AFC several times to address the claims they did not conduct a human rights assessment, and to get their response to the evolving situation involving the Iranian women's team.

FIFA only provided this statement earlier in the week.

"The safety and security of IR Iran's women’s national team are FIFA’s priority, and we therefore remain in close contact with the AFC and the relevant Australian authorities, including Football Australia, in relation to the team’s situation."

Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke pictured with the first five players who were granted humanitarian visas. (ABC News: Supplied: Department of Home Affairs)

Dr Catherine Ordway is a sport integrity expert and visiting scholar at UNSW's School of Business Canberra and said FIFA's silence is damning.

"[FIFA should tell the Iranian authorities] if you want to play at the FIFA Men's World Cup, then you need to ensure the safety of the players and their families, and that includes the Iranian women's team and their families," Dr Ordway said.

"But I haven't heard publicly that FIFA's making that statement. Perhaps there are negotiations going on privately behind the scenes, and I hope there are.

"If FIFA wants to award a peace prize to the President of the United States, Donald Trump, then they can also make statements in support of people who are being threatened with their lives.

"Just looking at this human rights issue alone, there is an obligation on FIFA, the Asian Football Confederation and the host, which is Football Australia, which owns the local organising committee for the Asian Cup, for them to have taken action to make sure that they protect the people that they invite for the tournament and that includes the Iranian team and their entourage."

Political forces in AFC

The safety of the Iranian women's team became a global talking point after they did not sing the national anthem before their first Asian Cup game against South Korea.

Players did not sing the national anthem before their first Women's Asian Cup game. (Getty Images: Albert Perez)

After being labelled "traitors" on Iranian state TV they sang and saluted during the anthem in their next two matches against Australia and Philippines, sparking fears that the players were being put under pressure to show deference to the state.

"It was very visible they are under too much pressure, they are under surveillance," Dr Khanjaninejad said.

It was alleged that the travelling Iranian entourage included people with ties to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), an organisation on the Australian government's list of state sponsors of terrorism since November.

The secret operation to get Iranian players alone After the increasing urgency over the fate of Iran's women's football team over the past few days, the breakthrough came last night, when five players were able to seek independent advice.

Human rights advocate and former Socceroo Craig Foster has been vocal this week, applying pressure on FIFA and the AFC to publicly comment and act.

"We understand football politics very well, Iran is a very powerful member of the Asian Football Confederation," he told ABC Radio Canberra.

"Expecting any sport politic body, whether it’s the IOC or FIFA or otherwise to act in these situations against powerful member federations is probably folly.

"Therefore the only leverage that can be applied two forms is internally by the Australian government, and publicly."

Players sang and saluted the national anthem before games against Australia and the Philippines. (Getty Images: Albert Perez)

The political webs are tangled in the AFC.

The AFC's president, Shaikh Salman bin Ebrahim Al Khalifa, is a member of Bahrain's royal family.

Hakeem al-Araibi, the Bahrain-born Australian footballer, who was held in Thai detention for several months, has previously criticised Shaikh Salman for not supporting the campaign to release him from detention.

At the time he said: "How can he [Shaikh Salman] be a 'fit and proper' leader for football in our region?"

The head of the Football Federation Islamic Republic of Iran, Mehdi Taj, is also one of the five AFC vice presidents.

He is a former IRGC commander and still believed to have strong links to the regime.

There is also a vice-president from Lebanon, which is currently involved in the war in the Middle East.

Lessons must be learned

There are doubts over whether Iran's men's team will compete in the upcoming men's World Cup to be hosted in North America from June.

The country's sports minister said Iran could not compete in the USA, after US forces launched air strikes alongside Israel.

There are doubts over whether Iran's men's team will travel to the USA for the World Cup in a few months. (Reuters: West Asia News Agency/Majid Asgaripour)

Dr Khanjaninejad says if they do end up participating, lessons must be learned from the Asian Cup.

"[FIFA] need to take proactive actions revealing how the risk was handled during the tournament and building a very formal protocol, step by step practical one, what they want to do in future cases for other athletes," she said.

"So they are not depending on ad hoc advocacy or public pressure or political intervention, which all happened in this case.

"They also need to think about athletes who are now returning to Iran who are going to be under so much pressure.

"How are they going to protect these athletes?

"I think we can highly anticipate that it's going to happen with the men's team as well, and what FIFA will do then, I don't know.

"Are they going to learn the lessons or not?"

title: Jason Day leads Australians at The Players Championship golf tournament
text:
Jason Day has rebounded from a dreaded double bogey at TPC Sawgrass's most iconic hole to lead the Australian challenge at the prestigious and mega-money Players Championship in Florida.

Day, the 2017 winner of the PGA Tour's flagship $US25 million ($35 million) event, carved out a two-under-par 72 to be three shots off the pace after the weather-hit first round.

Maverick McNealy, Lee Hodges, Sahith Theegala, Austin Smotherman and Austrian Sepp Straka share the lead at five under after rain forced a morning stoppage and late suspension of play at golf's unofficial fifth major championship.

While McNealy, Hodges, Theegala and Straka all posted 67s, darkness left Smotherman needing to return on Friday morning (Saturday AEDT) with a short birdie putt to reach six under and snare the solo first-round lead.

Two-time PGA Championship winner Justin Thomas is among four players in a tie for sixth just one stroke back on a cluttered leaderboard devoid of the sport's biggest names.

World number one Scottie Scheffler could only manage an even-par 72, while defending champion Rory McIlroy opened with a 74 to be tied for 69th after withdrawing from last week's Arnold Palmer Invitational with back spasms.

After missing two straight cuts since opening the year with a runner-up showing at The American Express, Day finally rediscovered some form a month out from the Masters.

Scrambling brilliantly, the former world No.1 conjured four birdies, his otherwise tidy round only tarnished by finding the water off the tee on the par-3 island hole.

In contrast, Adam Scott almost aced the 17th, only to pull his drive into the water at the last to rack up a double of his own in a round of 72.

The 45-year-old is making his 24th appearance at TPC Sawgrass, the most of any player in the field, and still seeking a second victory after saluting back in 2005.

Fellow Australian Min Woo Lee also carded a 72 despite opening the day with a double on the first hole.

Fresh off an impressive top 10 at Bay Hill, Lee dug deep with four birdies on the back nine to stay in the mix.

Karl Vilips also rallied late for a one-over 73, but Cam Davis's woes continued with a 77 as he battled to make the halfway cut.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

New Zealander Ryan Fox pulled out before the start of play, instead spending the day in hospital to have kidney stones removed.

Fox cited "illness" as the reason for his withdrawal and later posted to Instagram that this was hardly a case of the flu or a stomach virus.

"Not exactly the week I had planned," he wrote.

"Unfortunately, some kidney stones took me down on Tuesday night and then had to be dealt with surgically this afternoon.

"Devastated to miss out on such an amazing event; hoping to have a quick recovery and be back at it soon."

AAP

title: Pole vault legend breaks world record for a 15th time
text:
Swedish pole vaulter Armand "Mondo" Duplantis has broken the world record for the 15th time.

Duplantis cleared 6.31 meters at an indoor meet where he trains, which is named after him.

Loading Instagram content

The 26-year-old, who broke records at the world championships last year and the Paris Olympics the year before that, has eclipsed his mark by 1 centimetre every time since he first broke it in 2020.

This win at the Mondo Classic was his 38th straight pole vault victory — a stretch dating to August 2023.

Only Sergey Bubka has broken the record more times.

The Ukrainian great broke the outdoor record 17 times and the indoor record 18 times in the 1980s and 1990s.

Track and field's governing body changed the rules in 2000 to account for only one record, which can now be broken either indoors or outdoors.

Duplantis has three outdoor titles, along with two Olympic gold medals.

He is scheduled to jump next week at world indoors championship in Poland, where he will be going for his fourth straight title in that event.

AP

title: Pezet out to 'prove a point' after dicing Broncos in NRL upset
text:
Parramatta playmaker Jonah Pezet has destroyed the team he will play for next year — but in a sign of his appetite for excellence, he is far from satisfied.

The five-eighth's four try assists, and a try were key in sinking Brisbane 40-32 at Lang Park on Thursday night.

Next year, the 23-year-old will join the Broncos to replace retiring Adam Reynolds, but that is next year.

'Absolute madness': Broncos remain winless after Eels win bizarre NRL clash The Broncos' defence is labelled "pathetic" as they surrender a 20-6 lead, hit back in the second half but eventually lose 40-32 on the back of future teammate Jonah Pezet's game-sealing try.

Despite all of his great attacks, it was a try-saving tackle Pezet appeared most proud of.

Before the match, Broncos centre Kotoni Staggs said they would pressure Pezet by making sure second-rower Jordan Riki got in his face, like Melbourne's Joe Chan did the week before in the Storm's 52-4 demolition of the Eels.

The tactic failed. Instead, it was Pezet who made a try saver on Riki to jolt the ball free and stop the Broncos from taking a 16-0 lead.

It was a crucial play and one Pezet wants to do more often. He wasn't happy about missing a tackle, his only one, on Brisbane prop Payne Haas, that led to a try.

"It probably switched my mentality a little bit when I got physical, and actually used my shoulders for once," Pezet said.

"I need to keep doing that and take that into next week. That start I had tonight, I can't do that next week and for the next 25 rounds either. I need to start well and be physical from the start. I missed the jump tonight.

'I was pretty ordinary in the first 20 minutes to be honest. I let Payno go through untouched and I think I had an error early. I wasn't great, but that's footy — you just have to worry about the next moment and get on with it."

Pezet was brilliant at wiping away any disappointment quickly. He finished the opening half by setting up two tries and came up with a slick grubber in the second stanza that got the Eels the lead.

"When you make an error, you just have to get on with it and get on with my next job … the good players do," he said.

"My job is to help this team score points and take pressure off Mitchell [Moses].

"I guess in the last 60 minutes of the game I did that, but I need to start doing that for 80 minutes."

Pezet said it was "really special to get my first win for Parra" after the previous week's display.

"Everyone was pretty ordinary last week and I'll throw my hand up," he said.

"No-one was up to standard, and we had to come out here and prove a point and play the blue-and-gold footy we want to play."

AAP

title: Father jailed for neglect of 7yo son who died of pneumonia and sepsis
text:
A father who "prolonged his child's suffering" by failing to get him medical attention for 16 hours before his death, has been sentenced to 19 months in prison.

WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised that this article contains the name of a person who has died. Readers are also advised this story contains details that some may find distressing.

Shane McKenzie Wanganeen, 43, of Craigmore, previously pleaded guilty to causing harm to his seven-year-old son Makai through criminal neglect in February 2022.

He wept in the dock as the judge recounted details of the hours leading up to his son's death.
